title: Plumbing, Heating, and Air-Conditioning Contractors

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23822 ($100,763,014,046) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23822 ($306,277,104,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23822. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Heating, Air Conditioning, and Refrigeration Mechanics and Installers
First-Line Supervisors of Construction Trades and Extraction Workers
First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $260,272 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238220); class <5: 63,588 firms, avg revenue/firm $386,166 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,739 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 20,175 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,348,756 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $107,361 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 12,459 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,960,335 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $235,643 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 9,415 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,892,743 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $787,462 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,152 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,865,618 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,969,303 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 215 firms, avg revenue/firm $311,824,335 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $24,821,217 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,152

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Licensed physical trade work; AI does not turn wrenches. Only job documentation, permit paperwork and diagnostic assistance are exposed.
Physical install/service work with EPA 608 certification requirement. AI diagnostics and manuals-on-demand raise productivity modestly; substitution near zero.
Physical labor pipeline for the trades; unaffected by current AI except training delivery.
Crew coordination and QC are on-site; scheduling, job costing and progress reporting automate substantially but are a minority of the role's time.
Reporting, forecasting and admin automate; leadership, hiring and GC/key-account relationships persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Estimators / project managers (unlisted OEWS tail, est. 4% of wage bill)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Takeoffs, bid assembly and commercial estimating are heavily AI-tractable (ServiceTitan reports cost estimation as the top commercial AI use case at 24%); negotiation with GCs persists.
Lead qualification, quote generation and financing paperwork automate; in-home close of $10-20K replacement jobs stays human under current practice.
Invoicing, filing, data entry near-fully automatable with current AI embedded in FSM platforms.

breakdown_by_role.role: Dispatch, CSR, bookkeeping, permit clerks (unlisted OEWS tail, est. 12% of wage bill)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: The AI target zone: AI voice agents for call answering/booking are live in the industry now (documented end-to-end bookings without human involvement), dispatch optimization, invoicing, permit filing, renewals and review management automate near-fully; a residual human layer stays for escalation and complex scheduling.
Mixed drivers/laborers/misc trades in the unlisted tail; mostly physical, small admin sliver.

pi_dist.rationale: Residential service runs on flat-rate menu pricing (price-book per task), so AI-removed CSR/dispatch/admin cost is retained as margin in the short run rather than passed through hourly bills. But three leaks cap it: (1) lead-gen platforms (Google LSA/Angi) and OEM dealer programs own a growing share of demand and can raise per-lead pricing as contractor costs fall; (2) PE-scaled competitors deploying the same AI can re-anchor local price books downward over time; (3) the commercial/new-construction slice of the code is competitively bid against GCs and passes estimating savings through. Maintenance-agreement membership bases give partial client ownership but cover a minority of revenue for typical shops.

pi_moat.rationale: Strong structural barriers: state contractor licensing, permit requirements, EPA Section 608 refrigerant certification (and the A2L refrigerant transition), and equipment-warranty terms make homeowner DIY bypass via generic AI structurally marginal — AI can help self-diagnose but cannot legally or practically install/repair. Physical presence and truck-roll density are required. Deduction for smart-equipment self-diagnostics and OEM remote monitoring gradually thinning diagnostic service calls, and low switching costs between local contractors.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct ~25% of residential contractors 'currently using AI' but only ~12% with AI embedded in operations (ServiceTitan 2026) — call material adoption ~12-25% today. Momentum is unusually fast for a trade: ServiceTitan reports commercial contractors seeing measurable AI impact doubled from 17% (2025) to 38% (2026), and AI ships pre-embedded in the dominant FSM platforms, so adoption does not require a new software purchase. Against that, the historical analog (FSM software: ~15-20 years to ~50% mid-market; small firms still ~29%) and the 63,588 sub-5-employee firms argue for a long tail. Weighing the embedded-distribution channel against the small-firm tail: from ~15-25% to 50% material adoption in ~4 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: ServiceTitan press release / State of Home Services (residential), survey of 1,000 residential contractors conducted by Thrive Analytics, published 2026-04-07
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 74% view AI as 'an efficiency engine'; ~25% are 'currently using AI'; companion 2026 State of AI in the Trades: only 12% have embedded AI into their operations, 34% actively experimenting. Material = actively using AI in booking/dispatch/estimating/back-office functions; 0.25 is the 'currently using' upper reading, with embedded-material closer to 0.12.

historical_analogs.value: Field service management software: ~15-20 years from commercial availability to ~50% adoption in mid-market contractors (100-999 employees, ~51% today); small firms (<100 employees) still ~29% after two decades. Analog suggests slow trades diffusion, partially offset for AI because it ships inside the FSM platforms already installed.
historical_analogs.source: FieldServiceSoftware.io statistics compilation (secondary republication of industry survey data)
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Mid-market companies (100-999 employees) show adoption around 51%... small businesses (under 100 employees) have historically shown lower adoption at 29%, but this segment is growing fastest.

owners_60plus_pct.source: US Census Bureau (ABS-based visualization) via LeaguePark plumbing M&A commentary; no code-specific 60+ share published — ESTIMATE
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Cites Census data: 'the average small business owner in America is nearly 60 years old, and more than half are over 55'; 'Plumbing ownership skews even older, as many companies were founded by technicians who built strong regional brands in the 1980s and 1990s.' Basis for 0.30 owners 60+: if >50% are 55+ economy-wide and plumbing/HVAC skews older, ~30% aged 60+ is the central reading.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: LeaguePark (2026): 'Some hope to pass the business to family, but succession options are limited' — founders without internal successors are the documented driver of the current sale wave; the generational-shift-to-sale narrative is the industry's own M&A explanation.

active_consolidators.source: CT Acquisitions, 'Which Private Equity Firms Are Buying HVAC Companies in 2026?'
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: '18 verified active U.S. HVAC PE roll-up platforms' with publicly disclosed acquisitions Jan 2024-Apr 2026, incl. Apex Service Partners (~60 add-ons in 2025 alone), Sila Services, Service Logic, Wrench Group, Champions Group, Comfort Systems USA, Crete United, FirstCall Mechanical, Astra Service Partners, Redwood Services.

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions HVAC/plumbing M&A guides; corroborated by ClearlyAcquired (Q1 2025: residential HVAC $500K-$1M EBITDA averaged 6.3x)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Add-on/tuck-in transactions: '4-8x EBITDA depending on quality, recurring/membership-plan revenue mix, and geographic density' — midpoint 6.0x taken as median for single-location/small firms with normalized (post-owner-comp) EBITDA near or above $1M; true main-street sub-$1M-EBITDA shops trade lower (BizBuySell ~2.4-3.4x SDE).

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions PE guide; platform prints: Redwood Services ~$1.1B on $65M TTM EBITDA (~17x, Altas), Champions Group ~$2.5B (~18.5x, Blackstone)
exit_mult.figure_quoted: '7-12x EBITDA' for well-run growth-oriented platform-quality residential businesses with $2M+ EBITDA; recapitalization prints of ~17x (Redwood) and ~18.5x (Champions) at mega-platform scale. 12.0x taken as the credible exit for a $10M+ EBITDA platform, below the mega-platform prints.

pricing_structure.value: Residential service: flat-rate menu pricing (price-book per task) used by ~44% of companies and the fastest-growing model; remainder time-and-materials or hybrid. Residential replacement: fixed competitive per-install quotes ($10-20K tickets). Commercial/new-construction sub work: competitive cost-plus-ish bidding against GCs. Recurring membership/maintenance agreements cover a minority of revenue at typical shops.
pricing_structure.source: Profit Rhino (flat-rate survey); ServiceTitan HVAC pricing guide
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: '92% of 11,000 independently surveyed homeowners wanted a fixed price upfront (flat rate), but only 44% of companies charged using a flat rate.'

M.arithmetic: M = clamp(4 × (exit_mult - buy_mult) / buy_mult, 0, 10) = clamp(4 × (12.0 - 6.0) / 6.0, 0, 10) = clamp(4.0, 0, 10) = 4.0

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.0652 implies ~6.52pp of revenue freed at full adoption of the automatable slice — comfortably above the 3pp floor. A = 2.5 (< 5), so the uplift-vs-A tension test does not bind; a meaningful uplift with moderate adoption speed is internally consistent.

terminal_value.justification: The product is a licensed human physically installing/repairing equipment; mature AI cannot perform the act and state licensing, permits and EPA 608/A2L refrigerant law legally reserve it. AI is a cost-cutter on the ~15-20% office/sales/estimating layer, not a product-replacer. Demand-shape risks exist but net roughly neutral-to-positive: heat-pump electrification is a tailwind; smart-equipment self-diagnostics and OEM remote monitoring may thin diagnostic service calls and AI-armed national platforms may compress local margins — but paid demand for licensed on-site work does not shrink structurally. The ~57-60% trade-labor wage share simply caps how much AI can ever save this operator, which is a V ceiling, not a terminal-demand risk.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V was computed solely from the provided labor_share and role_mix plus declared within-role fractions grounded in task descriptions (licensing, physical work, office automation). A was derived from the ServiceTitan/Thrive adoption survey and the FSM-software historical analog. No deal activity, consolidation counts, or multiples informed V or A; PE evidence was used only in B (active_consolidators) and M (multiples).
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~44% of residential service companies on flat-rate menu pricing (growing), remainder T&M/hybrid; replacements quoted fixed per install; commercial work competitively bid against GCs. Flat-rate and fixed-quote pricing means AI savings are retained as margin short-run (no hourly pass-through), but competitive re-anchoring by AI-equipped PE platforms, lead-gen platform rent extraction (Google LSA/Angi), and the pass-through commercial bid segment erode this over time. Reflected in pi_dist = 0.55 (not higher despite flat-rate structure) and pi_moat = 0.75.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges 1.667-4.0; S ranges ~3.37-4.01 (S scales as (A/2.5)^0.2). Fast vendor-embedded AI push vs the 63,588-firm sub-5-employee tail is the single biggest swing on S.
M ranges ~1.71 (buy 7, exit 10) to ~6.4 (buy 5, exit 13); S ranges ~3.08-4.01. The multiple-arbitrage spread is poorly pinned because add-on and exit tiers overlap.
OW 0.529-0.957; B ranges ~5.34-7.18; S ranges ~3.56-3.78. No code-specific owner-age data exists; value is an estimate from economy-wide Census ABS plus 'skews older' commentary.
V ranges ~2.10-3.09; S ranges ~3.49-3.78. The unlisted OEWS tail (~26% of wage bill) forces estimated role sizes for dispatch/CSR and estimators.

reviewer_note: Heterogeneous code: 238220 blends residential service/replacement (scored — the dominant roll-up-relevant sub-segment), commercial mechanical, and cyclical new-construction subcontracting; electrical (238210), roofing (238160) and appliance repair (811412) are excluded neighbors, and association data (ACCA/PHCC) each cover only part of the code. Dataset conflicts used as given per rule 6: (1) labor_share 0.3508 is LOW quality, computed at ancestor 23822 with a 2024-wages/2022-receipts vintage mismatch; (2) n_band = 1,152 uses Damodaran's 7.96% Engineering/Construction margin, while residential service/replacement shops credibly run 10-20% EBITDA margins — at those margins many of the 9,415 firms in the 20-99 class ($9.9M avg revenue) are true $1-3M-EBITDA targets, so n_band and hence TD/B are likely understated; my research (ClearlyAcquired/CT Acquisitions margin commentary) supports the higher-margin view but the provided value was used. Adoption evidence is vendor-published (ServiceTitan) though third-party-fielded; owners_60plus_pct is an ESTIMATE for lack of code-specific data. Consolidation evidence (18 platforms, 17-18.5x mega prints) was used only for B and M. Formula outputs accepted without override; no intuition conflicts material enough to note beyond n_band understatement.
